answer:
# Declaring the two flags

In Cobra, **persistence** is controlled by which FlagSet you register on, not by anything on the flag itself.

```go
var verbose bool
var dryRun bool

var rootCmd = &cobra.Command{Use: "app"}
var childCmd = &cobra.Command{Use: "deploy", /* ... */}

func init() {
    // 1. Root-level flag visible to root AND every descendant:
    rootCmd.PersistentFlags().BoolVarP(&verbose, "verbose", "v", false, "verbose output")

    // 2. Flag visible only to `deploy` and its own subcommands:
    childCmd.PersistentFlags().BoolVar(&dryRun, "dry-run", false, "preview without executing")
    // (If you wanted dry-run on `deploy` ONLY — not even its grandchildren —
    //  use childCmd.Flags().BoolVar(...) instead.)

    rootCmd.AddCommand(childCmd)
}
```

Inside any handler — root, `deploy`, or a grandchild of `deploy` — you read it the same way: the bound variable, or `cmd.Flags().GetBool("verbose")`. After merging happens at execute time, the inherited persistent flag shows up in the child's resolved `Flags()` set.

# The four accessors on `*Command`

The cobra atlas (ADR-02) is explicit that a `Command` owns **five** internal pflag FlagSets — `flags`, `pflags`, `lflags`, `iflags`, and `parentsPflags` — and the four public accessors are windows onto different combinations of them:

| Method | What it returns | When you'd use it |
|---|---|---|
| `Flags()` | The **fully resolved** set: this command's local flags **plus** all persistent flags inherited from ancestors. This is what Cobra parses argv against and what your `Run`/`RunE` should read. | Reading flag values inside a command handler, or any time you want "what can the user actually pass here?" |
| `PersistentFlags()` | The FlagSet for flags **declared on this command that descendants will inherit**. ADR-02 notes this is a separate FlagSet from `Flags()`; entries only appear in `Flags()` after the merge that happens during `execute()`. | **Declaring** a flag you want children to see. This is where `--verbose` goes on `rootCmd` and `--dry-run` goes on `childCmd`. |
| `LocalFlags()` | A read-only view of the flags **defined on this command itself** (local + this command's own persistent), excluding ones inherited from a parent. The docstring stresses it doesn't mutate state — it's a current-state accessor. | Help/usage rendering ("flags for *this* command"), introspection. |
| `InheritedFlags()` | A read-only view of the flags **inherited from ancestors** only — i.e. persistent flags defined further up the tree. Also a pure accessor. | Help rendering ("Global Flags:" section), introspection. |

There's also a fifth accessor worth knowing about: **`LocalNonPersistentFlags()`** returns flags specific to this command that *won't* persist to subcommands — useful when you want to distinguish "my own knobs" from "knobs I'm exporting downward."

# Mental model

- **Where you register** decides reach: `Flags()` = this command only; `PersistentFlags()` = this command + all descendants.
- **Where you read** is almost always `cmd.Flags()` (or the bound variable), because that's the merged view the user actually saw on the command line.
- `LocalFlags()` and `InheritedFlags()` exist mainly so help text and doc generators (man/md/rst) can render the two halves separately — note that the atlas shows `InheritedFlags` is referenced from `doc/man_docs.go`, `doc/md_docs.go`, and `doc/rest_docs.go`, which is exactly the "render the Global Flags section" use case.

One subtlety from ADR-06 worth flagging if you ever group these: **flag groups (required-together, mutually-exclusive, etc.) only work when all the flags in the group are visible on a single Command after merging.** So you can group `--verbose` with a child-local flag on the child (because the child sees both via merge), but you can't group flags that live on different branches of the tree.

    RATIONALE "Nilable function fields model optional hooks with zero boilerplate in the common case."
  INTENT ADR-01 hard "Cobra's core type must be a concrete struct with public fields, not an interface."
    RATIONALE "Struct-oriented shape enables declarative literal construction, which is the dominant idiom in Go and determines usability at scale."
  INTENT ADR-01 hard "Deprecated fields remain on the struct rather than being removed or renamed."
    RATIONALE "Renames are breaking changes to every user; deprecation proceeds by addition."
  INTENT ADR-01 hard "Dispatch must be a nil check on the field followed by a direct function call, not a method call on an interface value."
    RATIONALE "Direct function-field invocation avoids interface vtable overhead and matches the data model."
  INTENT ADR-01 hard "Evolution of Command must proceed by adding new fields, not changing or hiding existing ones."
    RATIONALE "Public fields are part of the semantic contract; renaming or hiding breaks every user."
  INTENT ADR-01 hard "Moving field access behind methods, introducing a Commander interface, or adding a registration contract is a breaking redesign, not a refactor."
    RATIONALE "Such changes would force every user of cobra to rewrite their command definitions."
  INTENT ADR-01 hard "No Commander interface may be introduced for user command types."
    RATIONALE "Interface-based dispatch would force users into a second idiom and require optional-method detection via type assertions."
